title: Oops! Three Politicians Get Caught Betting on Themselves
summary: Imagine betting on yourself to win a race — and then getting in trouble for it. That's exactly what happened to three people running for Congress in the U.S. They used a website called Kalshi, where you can bet on things like elections. But Kalshi has a rule: candidates cannot bet on their own races. Now they've been fined (made to pay money as a punishment) and banned from the site for five years. One candidate, Mark Moran from Virginia, said he did it on purpose to show that these betting sites have too much power over elections. Another candidate, Matt Klein from Minnesota, bet just $50. He said it was a mistake and that he's sorry. A third candidate, Ezekiel Enriquez from Texas, lost his race with less than 2% of the vote. Some people think the fines are too small. A congressman named Mike Levin said, "That's not a punishment. That's a parking ticket." What do you think? Should politicians be allowed to bet on themselves?
why_it_matters: This story shows that even grown-ups in charge can make silly mistakes, and it makes you think about whether betting on elections is fair or dangerous.

questions.correct_answer: To show that betting sites have too much power over elections

background_read: Betting on elections is legal in some places but often has strict rules to keep it fair.
In the United States, candidates are usually not allowed to bet on their own races because it could create a conflict of interest.
Some people think politicians should not be involved in betting at all, while others say it's just for fun.
Article_Structure: WHO: Three candidates for Congress – Mark Moran, Matt Klein, Ezekiel Enriquez
WHAT: They bet on themselves using Kalshi and were fined and banned for five years
WHERE: The United States, on a website called Kalshi
WHY: Kalshi’s rule says candidates cannot bet on their own races; some candidates did it by accident or to make a point

perspectives.description: Matt Klein said it was a mistake and apologized. Mark Moran did it on purpose to show that betting sites have too much power.

perspectives.perspective: People who think the punishment is too weak
perspectives.description: Congressman Mike Levin called the fines 'a parking ticket' and thinks the penalty should be much harsher.
Kalshi has a rule banning candidates from betting on their own races and enforced it by fining and suspending the candidates.
